title: 2 Kings 23
subtitle: The Great Reform — and Its Limits

sections.header: Verses 1–20 — Covenant Renewed; Pagan Shrines Destroyed; Bethel Altar Demolished

refs.note: The man of God prophesied against Jeroboam’s altar: “A son named Josiah will be born to the house of David. On you he will sacrifice the priests of the high places.” Fulfilled here, by name, three centuries later.
“Destroy completely all the places where the nations you are dispossessing worship their gods.” Josiah finally executes what Moses commanded.

notes.note: Josiah’s reform teaches that no nation is beyond the reach of repentance, even if the consequences of past sin remain. The reform is genuine, the obedience is real, and God honours it — even though it cannot reverse the national judgment. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: topheth — the sacrificial site in the Valley of Hinnom south of Jerusalem. The name may derive from tōph (“drum”) — drums were beaten to drown out children’s cries during sacrifice. The defiled site later became Jerusalem’s rubbish dump, providing the imagery for Gehenna.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The “vessels made for Baal, Asherah, and the starry hosts” inside the temple confirm that Manasseh’s paganisation extended into the sanctuary itself. The reforms required physical purging of the temple — literally carrying out contaminated objects.

Horses dedicated to the sun god reflect Mesopotamian influence. Sun-worship was prominent in Assyrian religion, and Manasseh’s introduction of it was likely linked to his vassal status. Archaeological evidence from Tell Taanach includes cult stands with sun-disc imagery.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The fulfilment of the 1 Kings 13 prophecy is the Deuteronomistic historian’s most dramatic demonstration that the prophetic word controls history. Three hundred years pass, a king is born with the right name, and the word is executed. History is the unfolding of what God has spoken.

hist.context: Josiah launches the most comprehensive reform in Israel’s history. He reads the Book of the Law publicly, renews the covenant, then systematically destroys: the Baal and Asherah vessels in the temple, the idolatrous priests, the high places from Geba to Beersheba, the Topheth in the Valley of Hinnom (the child-sacrifice site), the horses dedicated to the sun god, the altars Manasseh built in the temple courts, and the high places Solomon built for foreign gods on the Mount of Olives. Then he crosses into former Israelite territory and destroys Jeroboam’s altar at Bethel — fulfilling the prophecy of the unnamed man of God from 1 Kings 13:2, spoken 300 years earlier.

heb.paragraph: Bayit (house) carries a deliberate double meaning in Chronicles: Solomon builds God a house (temple), and God promises David a house (dynasty). The interplay between these two 'houses' generates the theological tension that drives the entire narrative: will the dynasty endure? Will the temple remain? Both depend on covenant faithfulness.

notes.note: The devastating judgment recorded here was not arbitrary divine wrath but the long-warned consequence of persistent covenant violation. God had patiently sent prophet after prophet (2 Chr 36:15-16), but the people refused to repent. Believers must learn that God's patience, though immense, is not unlimited — He will discipline His people to restore them (Heb 12:5-11).

The theological framework of Kings evaluates every monarch by a single standard: fidelity to the covenant expressed in exclusive worship of Yahweh at the chosen place. Political success, military prowess, and economic prosperity are irrelevant apart from covenant obedience. This standard anticipates the NT principle that faithfulness to God's Word is the only measure of true success (Rev 2:10).

The judgment of exile was not the end of God's purposes but a severe mercy designed to purify and restore. God disciplines those He loves (Heb 12:6). For the believer, seasons of divine discipline are not evidence of God's abandonment but of His fatherly care. He prunes every branch that bears fruit so that it will bear even more (John 15:2).

sections.header: Verses 21–37 — Passover Restored; “Still the LORD Did Not Turn”; Josiah Killed; Final Kings

refs.note: “Love the LORD your God with all your heart, soul, and strength.” Josiah is the only king who fulfils the Shema completely — and it is still not enough.
Chronicles adds that Necho warned Josiah not to interfere, claiming divine commission. Josiah ignored the warning and was fatally wounded by archers.

notes.note: That the greatest reform cannot prevent judgment teaches the most important lesson in Kings: human obedience, however perfect, cannot atone for accumulated sin. Atonement requires something the law cannot provide. The OT narrative reaches its limit and points beyond itself.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The triple formula “heart, soul, strength” (lēbāb, nephesh, mĕʾōd) directly quotes Deut 6:5, the Shema. Josiah is the only king to receive this commendation. The irony is crushing: the one king who perfectly embodies the covenant’s demand cannot save the nation from the covenant’s curse.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Pharaoh Necho II (610–595 BC) was marching to support Assyria against the rising Babylonian-Median coalition at Carchemish. Josiah’s interception at Megiddo was likely an attempt to block Egyptian reinforcement of Assyria, aligning Judah with Babylon. The Battle of Megiddo (609 BC) is confirmed by Egyptian and Babylonian records.

Necho’s imposition of tribute (100 talents of silver, one talent of gold) and installation of Jehoiakim follows standard Egyptian imperial practice. Judah has traded Assyrian vassalage for Egyptian vassalage.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The “nevertheless” after the superlative verdict on Josiah is the theological hinge of the entire Deuteronomistic History. It declares that the covenant framework — obey and be blessed — has been so comprehensively violated that no amount of subsequent obedience can restore the balance. The system itself requires something beyond itself.

hist.context: Josiah celebrates a Passover greater than any since the judges — the narrator calls it unprecedented. The verdict seems to confirm his greatness: “Neither before nor after Josiah was there a king like him, who turned to the LORD with all his heart and soul and strength.” But then the devastating qualifier: “Nevertheless, the LORD did not turn away from the heat of his fierce anger... because of all that Manasseh had done.” Josiah’s faithfulness is real but the damage is irreversible. Then Pharaoh Necho marches north to support Assyria against Babylon; Josiah intercepts him at Megiddo and is killed. The “peace” promised by Huldah (22:20) is relative — he dies before the exile, not in it. Jehoahaz succeeds briefly before Egypt deports him and installs Jehoiakim.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew bĕrît (covenant) is the central relational term in the OT. In this context it underscores the binding obligation between God and His people — faithfulness brings blessing, unfaithfulness brings judgment. The Chronicler uses this term to evaluate every king's reign against the Davidic covenant standard.

notes.note: Every genuine revival in Israel's history was rooted in a return to God's Word. The pattern is consistent: rediscovery of Scripture leads to repentance, which leads to reformation of worship. This remains the pattern for spiritual renewal in every age — the church is reformed and revived only as it returns to the authority of God's written Word.

Every revival recorded in Scripture was initiated by God and sustained by obedience to His Word. The pattern is instructive for the modern church: spiritual renewal does not come through innovative programs or cultural accommodation but through humble return to the unchanging truth of Scripture. 'Will you not revive us again, that your people may rejoice in you?' (Ps 85:6).

ppl.name: Manasseh
ppl.text: This description should be attributed to Josiah, not Manasseh. Manasseh was known for promoting idolatry and undoing Hezekiah's reforms.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVJosiah brought all the priests from the towns of Judah and desecrated the high places, from Geba to Beersheba, where the priests had burned incense. He broke down the gateway at the entrance of the Gate of Joshua, the city governor, which was on the left of the city gate.ESVAnd he brought all the priests out of the cities of Judah, and defiled the high places where the priests had made offerings, from Geba to Beersheba. And he broke down the high places of the gates that were at the entrance of the gate of Joshua the governor of the city, which were on one\u2019s left at the gate of the city.NIVThe king ordered Hilkiah the high priest, the priests next in rank and the doorkeepers to remove from the temple of the Lord all the articles made for Baal and Asherah and all the starry hosts. He burned them outside Jerusalem in the fields of the Kidron Valley and took the ashes to Bethel.ESVAnd the king commanded Hilkiah the high priest and the priests of the second order and the keepers of the threshold to bring out of the temple of the LORD all the vessels made for Baal, for Asherah, and for all the host of heaven. He burned them outside Jerusalem in the fields of the Kidron and carried their ashes to Bethel.

themes.note: The Great Reform — and Its Limits

coaching.tip: Josiah's reform is the most thorough in Kings: altars demolished, Asherah poles burned, mediums expelled, high places defiled. The narrator says 'Neither before nor after Josiah was there a king like him.' Yet it is not enough — God's anger over Manasseh remains. The greatest reform in history cannot undo accumulated covenant violation.

prayer_prompt: As you consider Josiah's sweeping reforms — tearing down every high place, desecrating every altar — and the narrator's note that no king before or after turned to the LORD as he did, consider asking God whether radical obedience is something you admire in others or practice yourself.
